coredata.dc:title: MoS2−x/GCD-MoS2−x nanostructures for tuning the overpotential of Volmer-Heyrovsky reaction of electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Nayana, K.
Sunitha, A.P.
coredata.dc:description: MoS2 nanostructures are famous electrocatalysts in hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). The presence of graphene was already reported to boost the electrocatalytic activity of MoS2. This work reports the influence of Sulphur deficiency and economically synthesizable green carbon dot (GCD) incorporation into hydrothermally synthesized MoS2 nanostructures on the electrocatalytic activity of HER. All as-synthesized nanocatalysts are Sulphur deficient (MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x) and exhibited superior HER activity as compared to commercial MoS2. MoS2−x 220 exhibited a Tafel slope of 43 mV/Dec indicates the reaction mechanism as Volmer-Heyrovsky with Heyrovsky as rate determining step. Best electrocatalytic parameters are observed for the same catalyst with overpotential: 247 mV at 10 mA/cm2, Turnover frequency: 0.42 s−1, double layer capacitance: 23.8 mF/cm2 and electrochemically active surface area: 595/cm2. The obtained preferable HER parameters suggest that Sulphur deficiency and incorporation of GCD in MoS2 are playing a vital role in HER electrocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2 nanostructure
Green carbon dot
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Sulphur deficiency
Hydrothermal
Overpotential

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst misctext orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors would like to acknowledge Dr. K. J Saji, Associate Professor, International School of Photonics, CUSAT, Kochin-22, Kerala for providing characterization facilities. We also acknowledge Dr. Ambili Krishnan, Associate Professor, Department of Physics, and Dr. Shanthil. M, Assistant Professor, Department of Chemistry, Govt. Victoria College, Palakkad, Kerala and DST-FIST, Govt Victoria College, Palakkad for all the support and providing material synthesizing facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD of MoS2−x (b) XRD of GCD-MoS2−x nanostructures. (311) plane of carbon is evident in carbon dot incorporated MoS2−x (c–d) Raman spectra of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x. (c) Shows the presence of E1g, A1g and E2g vibrations of MoS2 and J1, J2 and J3 vibrations of 1T MoS2. In (d), G band is visible in all carbon dot incorporated catalysts.
(a–f) Size distribution of particles in MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x using dynamic light scattering analyzer at 80 ns gate time. Nanosized particles are confirmed by DLS.
FESEM images of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x. MoS2−x show scattered flower like structures.

EDX of nanostructured MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x. Amount of Sulphur deficiencies are estimated from the atomic percentage of elements, shown in EDX graph. For MoS2−x 180, MoS2−x 220 and GCD-MoS2−x 180, x = 0.6. For MoS2−x 140, GCD-MoS2−x 140 and GCD-MoS2−x 220, x = 0.8. Presence of carbon is observed in all carbon dot incorporated catalysts.

XPS of nanostrucutre MoS2−x 220. (a) Survey. Presence of Molybdenum and Sulphur are confirmed in survey (b) 3d energy level of Molybdenum. (c) 2p energy level of Sulphur. Peaks belong to 1T MoS2 and 2H MoS2 are there in both 3d energy level of Molybdenum and 2p energy level of Sulphur.
Absorption spectra of synthesized nanostrucutred MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x. In-site: tauc plots.

Upconversion PL of nanostrucutred MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x. Carbon dot incorporated MoS2−x have high intensity PL than MoS2−x. Variation of intensities of catalysts denote the variation in electron-hole recombination rate.

LSV analysis of prepared nanostructured MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x (a) Overpotential graphs of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x. At 10 mA/cm2 current density, Overpotentials are estimated and listed in Table 1. All as-synthesized catalysts show very low overpotential than commercial MoS2. MoS2−x 220 have obtained least overpotential of 247 mV (b) Variation of overpotential with carbon dot incorporation and temperature of synthesis. At 140 °C and 180 °C, overpotentials are reduced in carbon dot incorporation. At 220 °C, overpotential of MoS2−x is less than the GCD-MoS2−x. (c) Tafel plots of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x. (d) TOF versus overpotential graphs of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x.

Cyclic voltammetry of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x nanostructures (a) MoS2−x 140 (b) MoS2−x 180 (c) MoS2−x 220 (d) GCD-MoS2−x 140 (e) GCD-MoS2−x 180 (f) GCD-MoS2−x 220. In each graph, scan rates of 20 mV/s, 40 mV/s, 60 mV/s, 80 mV/s, 100 mV/s and 120 mV/s are used. Cdl and ECSA estimated from CV are listed in Table 2.

HER activity parameters of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x. Amount of Sulphur deficiencies and LSV parameters are shown. TOF are estimated at 300 mV, 350 mV and 400 mV overpotentials. MoS2−x 220 have obtained maximum TOF than other catalysts. As compared to commercial MoS2, all as-synthesized catalysts have better HER activity parameters.
HER activity parameters of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x nanostructures using CV. MoS2−x 220 have obtained maximum Cdl and ECSA.

head.ce:title: MoS2−x/GCD-MoS2−x nanostructures for tuning the overpotential of Volmer-Heyrovsky reaction of electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Government Victoria College, Affiliated to University of Calicut, Palakkad, Kerala 678001, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Government Victoria College, Palakkad, Kerala-678001, Affiliated to University of Calicut, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, N. S. S. College, Affiliated to University of Calicut, Ottapalam, Kerala 679103, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, N. S. S. College, Ottapalam, Kerala-679103, Affiliated to University of Calicut, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MoS2 nanostructures are famous electrocatalysts in hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). The presence of graphene was already reported to boost the electrocatalytic activity of MoS2. This work reports the influence of Sulphur deficiency and economically synthesizable green carbon dot (GCD) incorporation into hydrothermally synthesized MoS2 nanostructures on the electrocatalytic activity of HER. All as-synthesized nanocatalysts are Sulphur deficient (MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x) and exhibited superior HER activity as compared to commercial MoS2. MoS2−x 220 exhibited a Tafel slope of 43 mV/Dec indicates the reaction mechanism as Volmer-Heyrovsky with Heyrovsky as rate determining step. Best electrocatalytic parameters are observed for the same catalyst with overpotential: 247 mV at 10 mA/cm2, Turnover frequency: 0.42 s−1, double layer capacitance: 23.8 mF/cm2 and electrochemically active surface area: 595/cm2. The obtained preferable HER parameters suggest that Sulphur deficiency and incorporation of GCD in MoS2 are playing a vital role in HER electrocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy production by conventional methods leaves a high carbon footprint to the environment [1,2]. Chemical energy in the bonds of Hydrogen molecules can be utilized for carbon-free power production [3]. Conventional methods of Hydrogen gas production are steam reforming of natural gases and coal gasification [3,4]. Depending upon the value of carbon footprint, Hydrogen produced by different methods is categorized in different grades [3,4]. Brown hydrogen obtained from coal, grey Hydrogen obtained from natural gas and blue Hydrogen obtained by implementing carbon capture techniques in coal gasification and natural gas reformation are the main available grades of Hydrogen nowadays [3,4]. Green Hydrogen is another grade of Hydrogen for which the production methods follow carbon-neutral processes. There, the water splitting method and Hydrogen gas formation by a renewable energy source, a carbon-neutral process, have great significance [4]. Hydrogen produced by water electrolysis powered by a non-conventional energy source is perfectly green. Electrocatalysts play a vital role in the kinetics of hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in the splitting process of water by electrocatalytic method [5,6].

Electrocatalytic HER includes adsorption (Volmer reaction) and desorption (either Heyrovsky or Tafel reaction) of hydrogen ions on the active sites of the catalyst [7,8]. Desorption of hydrogen ions from the active sites followed by adsorption end up in hydrogen gas evolution [7]. The desorption of an adsorbed hydrogen ion by combining with one hydrogen ion present in the electrolyte is known as the Heyrovsky reaction. Adsorbed hydrogen ion reacts with hydrogen ion in the electrolyte and desorb from the catalytic site is known as the Heyrovsky reaction [4,7]. The process of combination of two adsorbed hydrogen ions is known as the Tafel reaction [4,8]. HER mechanism either opts Volmer-Heyrovsky or Volmer-Tafel reaction based on the thermodynamic potential of the adsorption/desorption reaction [4,7]. The slowest reaction is denoted as the rate-determining step in the reaction path. Even though a rate-determining step exists in HER, a balanced adsorption-desorption rate is essential for an efficient HER [7,8].

Suitable catalysts can be used to increase the reaction rate of Hydrogen evolution by electrocatalysis. The widely accepted electrocatalyst for HER is Platinum, which is a rare metal with high cost [9]. It necessitates the replacement of Platinum catalyst with a low-cost and efficient electrocatalyst. Because of the high surface to volume ratio and tunable properties, low-cost nanomaterial can be used as electrocatalysts instead of Platinum [10]. Nanostructures of Transition metal dichalcogenides, especially MoS2, reported having electrocatalytic properties in HER [11–16]. The active sites of nanoelectrocatalyst contribute electrons to hydrogen ions present in the electrolyte during HER [8,12]. By tuning the properties of electrocatalysts, the number of active sites can be improved [13,14].

The sufficient amount of active sites of MoS2 nanomaterial makes it suitable for HER [17,18]. Active sites in the MoS2 nanostructure adsorb hydrogen ions in the electrolyte. The two commonly appearing phases of MoS2 nanostructures are hexagonal (2H) and tetragonal (1T) phases. One unit cell of 2H MoS2 contains two MoS2 molecules, whereas 1T MoS2 has one molecule per unit cell. 2H MoS2 nanostructures are semiconducting with plenty of active edge sites [19]. 1T MoS2 nanostructures are metallic, whose basal planes, as well as edge sites, are electrocatalytically active. So, 1T MoS2 nanostructures can actively participate in HER catalytic action more than the 2H MoS2 [20,21]. Different strategies were adopted to elevate the number of active sites and catalytic activity of MoS2, like transforming the 2H phase to 1T, doping [20–23], fabricating heterostructures [24,25], making defects and disorders [21,26], etc. Optimized temperature and pressure of synthesis [27–29], doping or intercalation with metal atoms [30,31] are some methods adopted to increase the concentration of 1T phase of MoS2. Heterostructures of MoS2 with graphene [32–34], graphene oxide (GO) [34] and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [34,35] are reported with confirmed HER activity. Making defects [21,26] or strain [36] into the MoS2 crystals, making Sulphur deficiencies [37–39], making a very few layered MoS2 nanofims [40] and making MoS2 quantum dots [41,42] are some reported works that proved efficient HER activity.

It has been reported that Sulphur deficiency disturbs the inactive basal planes of 2H MoS2 and increases the intrinsic hydrogen adsorption sites in MoS2, resulting in fast kinetics of HER [37–39]. Sulphur deficiency promotes quick adsorption (Volmer) of hydrogen ions in HER [25,26]. Saleem et al. reported that in a Sulphur deficient MoS2, due to the presence of un-bonded Molybdenum atoms, Hydrogen ions can defuse more deeply into the catalyst, improving the HER rate [37]. A high extent of Sulphur deficiency adsorbs hydrogen ions strongly and inhibits the desorption mechanism. It necessitates the optimization of Sulphur deficiency.

High electrically conducting graphene and reduced graphene oxide were recognized to boost the electrocatalytic activity of MoS2 [43–45]. Graphene bonds MoS2 by van der Waal's force of attraction. Graphene helps in the smooth conduction of electrons. X Yu et al. reported that a uniform layer of graphene over MoS2 provides a large surface area and conductivity to enhance the electrocatalytic activity of MoS2 [32]. It is proved that the electrochemically active surface area of MoS2/graphene and MoS2/GO heterostructures are much better than the bare MoS2. An ensemble of artificially synthesized carbon dots and MoS2 is synthesized by R. C Vitoria et al. and proved electrocatalytic HER activity [46]. Cost-effective and eco-friendly synthesizable green carbon dots have the advantage of zero carbon footprint over artificially synthesizable carbon dots. Green carbon dots and graphene have similar electrical properties [47]. The consequence of electrocatalytic activity of the combination of green synthesized carbon dots and MoS2 is rarely studied. Eco-friendly precursors and cost-effective as well as simple synthesis methods make green carbon dots more favourable to use. Here, the influences of naturally synthesized carbon dots in the catalytic activity of Sulphur deficient MoS2 catalysts are examined. Sulphur deficient MoS2 (MoS2−x) and carbon dot incorporated MoS2−x are hydrothermally synthesized at three different temperatures.

Ammonium tetra thiomolybdate (ATTM) is used as precursor (Nice chemicals) for synthesizing MoS2. Hydrazine hydrate (Nice chemicals) is used as reducing agent. Ash guard juice is used to synthesis carbon dots.

0.025 M of ATTM is hydrothermally heated with 5 ml of hydrazine hydrate at 140 °C, 180 °C and 220 °C for 8 h, resulting samples labelled as MoS2−x 140, MoS2−x 180 and MoS2−x 220 respectively. Same hydrothermal method is repeated with ATTM and hydrazine hydrate along with 1 ml of carbon dot solution to get GCD-MoS2−x 140, GCD-MoS2−x 180 and GCD-MoS2−x 220 samples.

X-ray diffraction (Cu-Kα: wavelength 1.540598 Ao), Raman (Horiba Scientific, 532 nm laser), dynamic light scattering (Horiba Scientific SZ-100), Field effect scanning electron microscopy (JEOL-JEM 2100 F), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Omicron Nanotechnology Ltd, Germany, source: Mg-Kα with 1253.6 eV) and absorption studies (DT-3000 UV–Visible light source) are done to analyze the structural and size behavior of catalysts. Linear sweep voltammetry and cyclic voltammetry (CH Instrument: electrochemical analyzer) are done to examine the HER activity parameters.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x are shown in Fig. 1 (Fig. 1a and b). The position of peaks corresponding to (004), (101) and (110) planes of MoS2 are confirmed by JCPDS 37–1492 [48–50]. The peak at 20.8° belongs to the (311) plane of carbon (JCPDS 82–0505) [51,52]. Peaks near 22° in Fig. 1a and near 28° in Fig. 1b represent (110) and (021) planes of α-MoO3, respectively (JCPDS 05-0508) [53]. The hydrothermal method ensures high temperature and pressure during synthesis but not vacuum. So, with the growth of Sulphur deficient samples here, very small (in log scale) peaks corresponding to moO3 are found, may be due to small amount of oxidation. All carbon dot incorporated catalysts have the presence of (311) plane of carbon. The absence of the peak corresponding to (002) plane of bulk MoS2 at 14.5° confirms the nanostructure of catalysts [54].

Raman spectra of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x are shown in Fig. 1c. Out-plane vibrations (A1g) of Sulphur and in-plane vibrations (E2g) of Molybdenum and Sulphur are present in all MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x [55]. The separation between E2g (376 cm−1) and A1g (402 cm−1) is 26 cm−1, which denotes a few layered structures of catalysts [56]. MoS2−x 220 and MoS2−x 180 exhibit distinct peaks at 282.7 cm−1 belonging to E1g vibration. E1g denotes the in-plane vibration of the Sulphur atom [55]. In MoS2−x 220, a higher intensity of E1g than E2g suggests considerable defects in the catalyst [57]. A high temperature of synthesis at 220 °C may create defects in the catalyst [57]. The intensity ratio of E2g and A1g of MoS2−x 220 shows an odd behavior. The intensity of A1g is almost twice that of E2g for other catalysts. However for MoS2−x 220, both have nearly the same intensity. It may be due to the strain in the catalyst [58]. Along with the E1g, E2g and A1g vibrations, catalysts show the presence of 1T meta-stable state of MoS2 by the peaks corresponding to J1, J2 and J3 [59,60]. GCD-MoS2−x (Fig. 1d) exhibited peaks at 1360 cm−1 (D band of graphene) and 1580 cm−1 (G band of graphene). This leads to the confirmation of the presence of carbon dots in catalysts. A G band (SP2 hybridization) is a prominent characteristic Raman peak of graphite crystal. D band (sp3 hybridization) is highly related to defects in graphite crystal [61–63]. GCD-MoS2−x 140 and GCD-MoS2−x 180 have the presence of G band only. It shows that at 140 °C and 180 °C of hydrothermal heating, the defect level of GCD is very few [61,62]. Defect-free carbon dots enhance the physical interactions between MoS2−x to boost HER activity. But, GCD-MoS2−x 220 has exhibited both G and D bands. Hydrothermal heating of carbon dots at 220 °C is assumed to be reflected in defects of carbon dots [61,62]. Defect-rich carbon dots with high energy D band vibrations inhibit the catalytic activity of MoS2−x.
Hydrodynamic size of particles of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x are estimated by dynamic light scattering (DLS). The size of the particles is mostly found below 10 nm (Fig. 2 ).

In Fig. 3 , field effect scanning electron microscopic (FESEM) images of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x are shown. MoS2−x catalysts exhibited scattered flower-like structures. Energy dispersive x-ray (EDX) analysis of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x are shown in Fig. 4 . EDX gives the qualitative and quantitative estimation of the catalysts [63–66]. The elements present in the catalyst are obtained as peaks, which are positioned at the energies of emitted X-Rays during EDX analysis. Transfer of electron from M shell to L shell of Molybdenum gives the peak Lα (2.293 KeV). Similarly, Kα (17.5 KeV), Kβ (19.68 KeV) peaks of Molybdenum and Kα (2.308 KeV) peak of Sulphur are visible in the EDX plots [64–66]. Carbon incorporated catalysts have a peak at 0.2 KeV, which represents Kα transition of Carbon [64,65]. Quantitatively, Sulphur deficiency is estimated from EDX. MoS2−x 220, MoS2−x 180 and GCD-MoS2−x 180 obtained an approximate x value of 0.6. MoS2−x 140, GCD-MoS2−x 220 and GCD-MoS2−x 140 have estimated an approximate x value of 0.8 (Table 1 ).

XPS of MoS2−x 220 is plotted in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a denotes the presence of Molybdenum and Sulphur in MoS2. In Fig. 5b, the binding energies of 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 energy levels of Molybdenum (2H MoS2) are located at 232.15 and 235.7 eV, respectively. Peaks at 228 and 232.75 eV denote the presence of Molybdenum corresponding to 1T MoS2 [67–69]. 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 energy levels of Sulphur (2H MoS2) peaked at 163.5 and 164.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 5c). Sulphur also exhibited peaks corresponding to 1T phase of MoS2 (2p3/2 at 160.2 and 2p1/2 at 161.8 eV) [63–65]. XPS analysis shows that about 38 % of Molybdenum and 42 % of Sulphur belong to 1T MoS2. Molybdenum: Sulphur ratio derived from Fig. 5b and c is 1.52. It confirms the Sulphur deficiency of MoS2−x 220 (x = 0.48).

Absorption spectra of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x catalysts are plotted in Fig. 6 . GCD-MoS2−x have more promising broad-range absorption spectra than MoS2−x. It may be due to the multi-absorption sites present in the GCD-MoS2−x under carbon dot incorporation. At 180 °C and 220 °C, 20 % of absorption and at 140 °C, 15 % of absorption of the carbon dot incorporated catalysts are prolonged up to 900 nm of wavelength. An extra hump of absorbance (320 nm) is noticeable for GCD-MoS2−x 220 (Fig. 6c) may be due to the presence of defect-rich carbon dots in the catalyst. Tauc plots to derive direct band gap energy are shown in-site of respective absorption spectra [17].

Upconversion photoluminescence (PL) of MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x is shown in Fig. 7 . For 630 nm (1.97 eV) of wavelength of excitation, catalysts exhibited a PL peak at 473 nm (2.62 eV). An increase in intensity is noted for carbon dot incorporated catalysts. GCD-MoS2−x 140 and GCD-MoS2−x 180 have observed maximum intensity. The intensity of GCD-MoS2−x 220 is much less than the other two carbon dot incorporated catalysts. The high intensity of PL indicates a high radiative electron-hole recombination rate [70]. The influence of carbon dots on radiative electron-hole recombination is very evident from the PL spectra. The defects of carbon dots present in GCD-MoS2−x 220 may ruined the ability of upconversion PL of it.

Electrochemical analysis for deriving HER activity parameters is done by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV). Three electrode water-splitting cell is used for LSV and CV. A glassy carbon electrode with a 3 mm diameter is taken as the working electrode. Platinum wire is taken as the counter electrode. Ag/AgCl is used as the reference electrode. All potentials are calibrated to the potential of the reduced hydrogen electrode by the Nernst equation [71]. 0.5 M H2SO4 is used as an electrolyte. Overpotential, Tafel slope and turnover frequency (TOF) are calculated from LSV. Double layer capacitance (Cdl) and electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) are estimated from cyclic voltammetry.

Overpotential is the potential to be applied to draw an amount of current density during HER [72]. The overpotentials (at a current density of 10 mA/cm2) of catalysts are shown in Fig. 8 a. Commercial MoS2 has obtained an overpotential of 545 mV/cm2. The dependence of Sulphur deficiency on overpotential is noticed here. MoS2−x 220, GCD-MoS2−x 180 and MoS2−x 180, which have a Sulphur deficiency of 0.6, have estimated less overpotential than other catalysts (x = 0.8). The least overpotential value is observed for MoS2−x 220 as 247 mV. Sulphur deficiency may improve the number of active sites in MoS2 nanostructures [25,26]. These active sites can adsorb hydrogen ions due to low Gibb's free energy. It may result in faster adsorption of hydrogen on the catalyst (Volmer reaction) [25,26]. For MoS2−x 140, GCD-MoS2−x 220, and GCD-MoS2−x 140, Sulphur deficiency is very high (x = 0.8), which may lead to the strong binding between adsorbed hydrogen and catalytic site. Strong hydrogen binding declines the desorption process and, hence, the total reaction rate of HER.

The dependency of overpotential on carbon dot incorporation and temperature is shown in Fig. 8b. At 140 °C and 180 °C, the presence of carbon dots enhances the HER activity. At 220 °C, the bare catalysts exhibited better HER activity than the carbon dots incorporated MoS2−x. As per the Raman spectra of MoS2−x 220, the strain formed in the catalyst may change the density of states and increases the carrier mobility; it can cause the enhancement in the intrinsic active sites of the catalyst [36]. Lee et al. reported that the strain can enhance the d-state density of Molybdenum atoms that supply more electrons for catalytic activity [36]. So, the availability of Volmer-Heyrovsky reaction sites increases, which enhances the catalytic activity of the nanocatalyst. Carbon dots incorporation supports the electrocatalytic character of MoS2−x by contributing high electrical conductivity to the nanostructure [13,25–29]. However at 220 °C, the presence of defect-rich carbon dots may reduce the electrical conductivity and, hence, the electrocatalytic activity of GCD-MoS2−x 220.

In acidic electrolytes, adsorption of hydrogen on the catalytic site (Volmer reaction) and desorption of hydrogen either by Heyrovsky or Tafel reaction are the three mechanisms involved in HER. Adsorbed hydrogen and hydrogen ions combine to form hydrogen molecules, known as the Heyrovsky reaction. The combining of two adsorbed hydrogen ions is known as the Tafel reaction. HER either follows the Volmer-Heyrovsky reaction or the Volmer-Tafel reaction. For fast HER, hydrogen adsorption, as well as desorption rates, should be engineered and optimized. Electrocatalysts can influence the reaction mechanisms of HER and significantly affect its kinetics [9,11,12].

Tafel slope denotes the rate of change of applied potential with respect to current density [73,74]. The Butler-Volmer equation for total cathodic and anodic current density I is
(1)
I
= 2
I 0
(
−
e
α z η F
R T
+
e
β z η F
R T
)

Here the first term denotes the cathodic current due to HER. I0, η, F, R and T are the exchange current density, overpotential, faraday's constant, universal gas constant and temperature, respectively. α and β are the transfer coefficient of the cathode and anode, respectively [74]. Usually, both α and β are taken as 0.5 [75]. If the Volmer reaction is the rate-determining step (slowest reaction) of either the Volmer-Heyrovsky or the Volmer-Tafel reaction mechanisms, cathodic current in Eq. (1) becomes
(2)
I
= 2
I 0
(
−
e
α η F
R T
)

If Heyrovsky is the rate-determining step of the Volmer-Heyrovsky reaction, the Butler-Volmer equation reduces to
(3)
I
= 2
I 0
(
−
e
−
(
1 +
α
)
η F
R T
)

Tafel slope obtained from Eq. (2) is
(4)
− 2.303
R T
α F

Tafel slope for Eq. (3) is
(5)
− 2.303
R T
(
1 +
α
)
F
Value of Eqs. (4) and (5) at 298 K are 120 mV/Dec and 40 mV/Dec, respectively [75].

If the Tafel slope is in between 40 mV/Dec. and 120 mV/Dec. the rate-determining step will be Heyrovsky, where hydrogen adsorption will occur faster than desorption. If the Tafel slope is above 120 mV/Dec., the rate-determining step will be Volmer, resulting in faster hydrogen desorption than adsorption. If the Tafel slope is below 40 mV/Dec, the Tafel reaction will be the rate-determining step, which is the most preferred reaction mechanism for fast HER. Platinum offers a reaction mechanism with a Tafel slope of about 30 mV/Dec [75,76]. Here (Fig. 8c), the minimum Tafel slope is exhibited by MoS2−x 220 (43 mV/Dec.). Among as-synthesized catalysts, maximum Tafel slope is observed for MoS2−x 140 (73 mV/Dec.). It suggests MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x follow the Volmer-Heyrovsky reaction mechanism in HER with Heyrovsky as the rate-determining step. As discussed before, Sulphur vacancies in MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x can easily adsorb hydrogen ions with less Gibb's free energy to fasten the Volmer reaction.

Turnover frequency (TOF) denotes the rate of hydrogen molecules evolved per catalytic site. It can be estimated by
(6)
T O F
=
I
2
F n
where I is the current density belonging to a particular overpotential in LSV [77,78]. The number of moles of catalyst participating in HER is taken as n. Here, TOFs are estimated for current densities at 250 mV, 300 mV, 350 mV and 400 mV of overpotentials (Table 1). TOF of catalysts follows the same hierarchy in catalytic activity as that of overpotential. MoS2−x 220 has obtained maximum TOF at an overpotential, whereas commercial MoS2 has obtained minimum. Fig. 8d shows that the as-synthesized catalysts with lesser overpotential have a faster increase in TOF with respect to overpotential. TOF of commercial MoS2 is very weak over the whole overpotential range. At −250 mV overpotential, the TOF of MoS2−x 220 is 10 times greater than the TOF of commercial MoS2. At −400 mV overpotential, the TOF of the same catalyst is fastly grown to 84 times than the TOF of commercial MoS2. Meanwhile, at −400 mV overpotential, the TOF of MoS2−x 140 has observed a hike in TOF of 46 times of the commercial MoS2. It is understandable from the TOF that the amount of Sulphur deficiency, the temperature of synthesis of catalysts and carbon dot incorporation play a significant role in the hydrogen production rate. Better TOF are evident for x = 0.6. At 180 °C and 140 °C, carbon dots incorporation leads to better TOF. Incorporated carbon dots may contribute to the electrical conductivity of MoS2−x to enhance catalytic activity. At 220 °C, MoS2−x exhibited better TOF than GCD-MoS2−x. Here, the defect-rich carbon dots may hinder catalytic activity.
Linear sweep voltammetry parameters are listed in Table 1.

In a water electrolysis cell, there are two types of capacitance exists. Capacitance formed at the electrode-electrolyte interface is known as Stern layer capacitance [79]. At electrolyte, a diffused layer capacitance exist [79]. According to the Stern-Gouy-Chapman model of capacitance, the effective capacitance of Stern's layer and diffused layer capacitances in water-splitting cells is known as double layer capacitance (Cdl) [79]. For a highly concentrated electrolyte, the value of diffused layer capacitance contributes nothing to the effective capacitance. So, Cdl represents mainly the capacitance at the electrode-electrolyte interface (Stern layer) [79,80]. At the electrode-electrolyte interface, the electrode and the ions present in the electrolyte (attracted towards the electrode) constitute the two conducting plates of Stern layer capacitance. Solvent molecules between the two conducting plates act as a dielectric medium [80]. So, Cdl is proportional to the active charges of the catalyst coated on the electrode. Cdl can be written as
(7)
C
d l
=
Q
p w
where pw is the potential window used in CV (difference between maximum and minimum potentials applied) and Q is the active charges participating in catalytic activity. So the value of Cdl indicates how much of intrinsic active edges are present in the catalyst.

Electrochemically active surface area is estimated by
(8)
E C S A
=
C
d l
C
s p
where Csp is the specific capacitance (40 μF) [81]. Since the intrinsic catalytic activity depends more on ECSA than the geometrical area, the estimation of ECSA puts light on the efficiency of the nanocatalysts towards HER. The catalytic activity of nanostructures is getting enhanced due to the high surface to volume ratio. When the surface to volume ratio increases, its ECSA, which indicates the interaction cross section of catalyst in electrolysis, is also increases even though the geometrical surface area remains constant. Thus, for an efficient electrocatalyst, ECSA should be high.

Electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution activities of hydrothermally grown Sulphur deficient MoS2 (MoS2−x) and green carbon dot incorporated MoS2−x nanostructures prepared at different temperatures-140 °C, 180 °C and 220 °C, are investigated in this work. MoS2−x exhibited scattered flower-like structures. EDX show Sulphur deficiency in catalysts. An optimum Sulphur deficiency of x = 0.6 is noted to have better catalytic activity. All MoS2−x and GCD-MoS2−x catalysts follow the Volmer-Heyrovsky reaction mechanism, with Heyrovsky as the rate-determining step in HER. Carbon dot decorated catalysts at 140 °C and 180 °C have better kinetics than the MoS2−x. At 220 °C, the reaction kinetics of the bare sample (MoS2−x 220) is greater than the carbon dots decorated. Among the prepared catalysts, MoS2−x 220 has obtained a minimum overpotential of −247 mV at 10 mA/cm2 current density and Tafel slope of 43 mV/Dec to fasten the Volmer-Heyrovsky reaction of HER. At an overpotential of −400 mV, the TOF of MoS2−x 220 is 84 times greater than the TOF of commercial MoS2. Even though MoS2−x 140 has obtained the least HER activity among synthesized nanostructures of MoS2, its HER parameters are far better than the commercial MoS2. At an overpotential of −400 mV, the TOF of MoS2−x 140 is 46 times greater than the TOF of commercial MoS2. To sum up, this work studies the effect of synthesis temperature, amount of Sulphur deficiency and decoration with carbon dot on the electrocatalytic activity of MoS2 nanostructures. Green carbon dot incorporation as suggested by this article, opens up a new method to enhance the HER activity of MoS2.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

We also acknowledge Dr. Ambili Krishnan, Associate Professor, Department of Physics, and Dr. Shanthil. M, Assistant Professor, Department of Chemistry, Govt. Victoria College, Palakkad, Kerala and DST-FIST, Govt Victoria College, Palakkad for all the support and providing material synthesizing facilities.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 2D transition metal dichalcogenides: design, modulation, and challenges in electrocatalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Fu Q, Han J, Wang X, Xu P, Yao T, Zhong J, Zhong W, Liu S, Gao T, Zhang Z, Xu L. 2D transition metal dichalcogenides: Design, modulation, and challenges in electrocatalysis. Advanced Materials. 2021 Feb;33(6):1907818.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual‐functional N dopants in edges and basal plane of MoS2 nanosheets toward efficient and durable hydrogen evolution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xiao, Wen, et al. "Dual-functional N dopants in edges and basal plane of MoS2 nanosheets toward efficient and durable hydrogen evolution." Advanced Energy Materials 7.7 (2017): 1602086.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non‐noble metal‐based carbon composites in hydrogen evolution reaction: fundamentals to applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang J, Xu F, Jin H, Chen Y, Wang Y. Non-noble metal-based carbon composites in hydrogen evolution reaction: fundamentals to applications. Advanced materials. 2017 Apr;29(14):1605838.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Accurate simulations of electric double layer capacitance of ultramicroelectrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, Hainan, and Laurent Pilon. "Accurate simulations of electric double layer capacitance of ultramicroelectrodes." The Journal of Physical Chemistry C 115.33 (2011): 16711-16719.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-dimensional CoS2–MoS2 nano-flakes decorated MoO2 sub-micro-wires for synergistically enhanced hydrogen evolution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, Yang, et al. "One-dimensional CoS2-MoS2 nano-flakes decorated MoO2 sub-micro-wires for synergistically enhanced hydrogen evolution." Nanoscale 11.8 (2019): 3500-3505.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: How do pseudocapacitors store energy? Theoretical analysis and experimental illustration

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Costentin, Cyrille, Thomas R. Porter, and Jean-Michel Savéant. How do pseudocapacitors store energy? Theoretical analysis and experimental illustration. ACS Appl. Mater. Interf. 9.10 (2017): 8649-8658.
